chain.label: Diaphragm
chain.connection: via shared L1–L3 vertebral attachments — crural contraction tensions the anterior vertebral bodies, altering psoas's mechanical environment

chain.label: Psoas
chain.connection: increased lumbar lordosis changes iliacus line of pull, biasing the ilium toward anterior rotation

chain.label: Iliacus
chain.connection: L innominate tilts forward (L IP ER = L AF ER); femur is in relative ER, adductor relatively lengthened, TFL loaded as compensatory IR stabilizer

chain.label: TFL
chain.connection: overloaded as compensatory frontal plane stabilizer; tension transmits via IT band

chain.label: Vastus Lateralis
chain.connection: lateral overload fails to stabilize; BF recruited as terminal compensator

diaphragm.role: Drives respiration. In L AIC, the left hemidiaphragm loses its Zone of Apposition (ZOA) — it flattens, becomes tonic, and shifts from a phasic respiratory muscle to a postural one. Crural tension on L1–L3 increases lumbar lordosis. (Manual p.2)
diaphragm.pattern: L diaphragm flattens → ZOA lost → diaphragm becomes tonic/postural rather than phasic/respiratory → crural pull increases lumbar extension bias → tensions the anterior vertebral bodies at shared L1–L3 attachment sites, altering psoas's resting mechanical environment. (Manual p.2; crural attachments per Gray's Anatomy, Standring 2016)
diaphragm.correction: Restore L ZOA via 90-90 Hip Lift with Balloon. The corrective mechanism is exhalation — active exhalation against balloon resistance re-domes the diaphragm and restores ZOA. The 90-90 position slackens the psoas and posteriorly tilts the pelvis; the balloon ensures the diaphragm domes rather than the patient simply holding their breath. (Manual p.45, 74)
diaphragm.context: The left hemidiaphragm specifically loses ZOA because it lacks the liver as a platform. The right hemidiaphragm sits on the liver and has a stable base for contraction. Without that support, the left flattens and becomes tonic. This asymmetry is why PRI considers L AIC the baseline pattern in nearly everyone. (Manual p.2)
diaphragm.hierarchyRole: Inhibited during repositioning. Not directly facilitated.

psoas.role: Primary hip flexor and lumbar spine stabilizer. Originates at T12–L5 vertebral bodies, intervertebral discs, and transverse processes; inserts at the lesser trochanter (StatPearls: Psoas Major, NBK535418; Gray's Anatomy, Standring 2016). In L AIC, PRI describes the left psoas as shortened, increasing lumbar lordosis and forward-rotating the left side of the lumbar spine. (Manual p.2)
psoas.pattern: L psoas shortens → L lumbar lordosis increases → iliacus line of pull shifts, biasing the ilium toward anterior rotation (L IP ER). Psoas shortening contributes to anterior pelvic tilt, which lengthens and mechanically disadvantages the left IO/TAs — removing the primary posterior tilt check on the system. PRI presents this as a direct inhibitory mechanism; anatomically, the effect is indirect and positional: psoas tips the pelvis, the tilt lengthens the abdominal wall, and lengthened IO/TAs lose mechanical advantage. (Manual p.2–3; IO/TA relationship is PRI interpretation)
psoas.correction: Inhibited indirectly via the three L AIC repositioning exercises: (1) 90-90 Supported Hip Lift with Hemibridge, (2) Right Sidelying Respiratory Left Adductor Pull Back, (3) Left Sidelying Right Glute Max. The 90-90 position mechanically slackens the psoas by reducing lumbar lordosis and posteriorly tilting the pelvis. There is no direct psoas inhibition exercise in the L AIC program — position does the work. (Manual p.45, 74)
psoas.context: Psoas does not attach to the ilium. It contributes to anterior pelvic tilt by increasing lumbar lordosis, which tips the pelvis forward; the iliacus is the direct ilium mover. PRI frames psoas shortening as directly elongating and inhibiting the left IO/TAs. The mechanism is indirect — psoas contributes to the anterior tilt that lengthens the abdominal wall, and lengthened IO/TAs lose mechanical advantage. This is a positional chain, not a muscle-to-muscle inhibitory pathway. (Manual p.2–3; mechanism distinction is anatomical, not PRI)
psoas.hierarchyRole: Inhibited during repositioning. Not directly facilitated in isolation.

iliacus.role: Hip flexor originating from the iliac fossa; inserts with psoas at the lesser trochanter. In L AIC, the left iliacus anteriorly rotates the left innominate — PRI considers it the direct driver of L IP ER. (Manual p.2, 44)
iliacus.pattern: L iliacus pulls ilium forward → L IP ER (anterior tilt, abduction, ER of innominate) → acetabulum rotates, passively orienting femur inward via geometry → TFL overloads in response. (Manual p.2)
iliacus.correction: Left proximal iliacus is a direct facilitation target at Step 3 of the L AIC hierarchy. Exercises: Right Sidelying Supported Left Glute Med; Right Sidelying Hemi 90-90 with Left FA IR variations. The proximal fibers act on the ilium in closed chain to produce inlet adduction (IP IR), working alongside left gluteus medius. Note: PRI calls this muscle 'anterior glute med' — it is still iliacus, not gluteus medius. (Manual p.44, 76)
iliacus.context: Iliacus has both a pathological role (driving L IP ER in the pattern) and a corrective role. The proximal iliacus, when the femur is fixed, pulls the ilium medially — inlet adduction (IP IR). PRI's label 'anterior glute med' for this action is clinically idiosyncratic but refers specifically to the proximal iliacus fibers. Distal iliacus is part of the problem; proximal iliacus is part of the solution. (Manual p.44, 76)
iliacus.hierarchyRole: Pathological: drives L IP ER. Corrective: left proximal iliacus facilitated at Step 3 (inlet adduction, IS ER). Test marker: −R PART achieved at Step 3, R HALT 2/5.

tfl.role: Tensor fasciae latae. Standard actions: hip abduction, hip flexion, and hip internal rotation (StatPearls: TFL, NBK499870; Kenhub: TFL). In L AIC, PRI describes TFL as overactive — recruited as a compensatory frontal-plane stabilizer because left glute med is inhibited (abduction failure) and left IO/TAs are weak (pelvic stability failure). PRI emphasizes TFL's frontal-plane compensation (abduction). However, TFL also produces hip IR — particularly when the hip is flexed (Kenhub: TFL). Dismissing TFL's rotational contribution oversimplifies its role in the pattern. (Manual p.2; PRI's frontal-plane emphasis is selective)
tfl.pattern: Left glute med inhibited → TFL recruited as compensatory abductor to maintain frontal plane stability. Separately, left IO/TA weakness reduces pelvic stability, further increasing TFL demand as a frontal-plane stabilizer. TFL tension transmits via iliotibial (IT) band to vastus lateralis → lateral knee loading increases. (Manual p.2; Physiopedia: TFL)
tfl.correction: Inhibited via L AIC Inhibition Step 1 (inhibit left anterior inlet): 90-90 Respiratory Left Hip Shift. This promotes left IP IR (extension, adduction, IR of the inlet), directly opposing the abduction bias that TFL sustains. TFL is not facilitated. (Manual p.77–78)
tfl.context: PRI emphasizes TFL's frontal-plane overload (abduction compensation). However, TFL also produces hip IR, particularly in flexion — its rotational contribution is position-dependent and should not be dismissed (StatPearls: TFL, NBK499870). TFL compensates for glute med's failure as an abductor and for IO/TA weakness as a pelvic stabilizer — two distinct mechanisms that PRI groups together. Its effect on VL is transmitted via the IT band; without that anatomical link the TFL → VL relationship in the chain would be arbitrary. (Manual p.2; mechanism separation is anatomical, not PRI)
tfl.hierarchyRole: Inhibited as a compensator via L AIC Inhibition Step 1. Not facilitated.

vastus_lateralis.role: Largest quadriceps component. Extends the knee with a lateral pull vector due to its oblique fiber orientation. PRI describes VL as an 'overactive, compensatory antagonistic hip stabilizer' (Manual p.2). VL does not cross the hip joint — it acts at the knee. PRI's claim is that VL's distal femoral control indirectly stabilizes the hip; this is plausible but imprecise.
vastus_lateralis.pattern: IT band tension from TFL transmits to VL via their shared lateral fascial connection → VL lateral pull vector increases. PRI interprets VL overactivity as compensatory hip stabilization. Since VL acts at the knee, this interpretation requires that distal femoral control indirectly supports hip stability — plausible via the IT band and lateral fascial system, but not a direct hip-stabilizing action. (Manual p.2; joint-crossing distinction is anatomical)
vastus_lateralis.correction: No dedicated VL inhibition exercise. Addressed solely by resolving the proximal chain: the same three L AIC repositioning exercises that inhibit psoas (90-90 Supported Hip Lift with Hemibridge; Right Sidelying Respiratory Left Adductor Pull Back; Left Sidelying Right Glute Max). Restoring femoral IR via pelvic correction reduces VL demand. VL is not facilitated. (Manual p.45, 74)
vastus_lateralis.context: VL's lateral pull is a function of fiber orientation, not femoral rotation position. Even with the femur in FA IR, VL's oblique fibers still create a lateral patellar force vector. PRI frames VL overactivity as a sign that glute med and IO/TAs have failed. VL cannot replicate glute med's hip abduction or IO/TA's trunk stabilization — its contribution is limited to distal femoral control via knee extension and lateral patellar tracking. (Manual p.2; joint-crossing limitation is anatomical, not PRI)
vastus_lateralis.hierarchyRole: Inhibited as a compensator. Not facilitated.

biceps_femoris.role: Long head: hip extension and knee flexion with tibial external rotation. Tibial ER is BF's standard action at the knee — it inserts on the fibular head and lateral tibial condyle, pulling the lateral tibia posteriorly during knee flexion (standard anatomy). Terminal muscle in the L AIC chain. PRI describes BF as firing as a compensator because the pelvis is anteriorly rotated over the left femur (L AF ER) — BF uses hip extension from below to resist forward pelvic collapse. (Manual p.2)
biceps_femoris.pattern: L AF ER (pelvis forward over femur) → BF fires to resist forward collapse via hip extension. BF produces tibial ER whenever it contracts — this is its standard mechanical output, not a special pathological behavior. PRI's contribution is explaining why BF may be overactive (compensating for proximal chain failure), which would increase tibial ER magnitude. The clinical sign of foot-out reflects the net sum of rotations across the hip and knee: femoral ER from deep external rotators (piriformis, etc.) compounds with BF's tibial ER at the knee — both push the foot outward. Isolating BF as the sole explanation for foot-out requires controlling for hip rotation. Note: femur is in FA IR at the hip; tibial ER from BF is at the knee — different joints, different observations. Students who conflate these will misread the clinical presentation. (Manual p.2; rotational compounding is anatomical, not PRI)
biceps_femoris.correction: BF is inhibited as a compensator. The corrective hamstring target is medial hamstrings (semimembranosus and semitendinosus) — they produce knee flexion with tibial IR, directly countering BF's tibial ER bias. The 90-90 Supported Hip Lift with Hemibridge engages the hamstring group for repositioning; in PRI, 'corrective hamstring work' always refers to medial hamstrings, never BF itself. No dedicated medial hamstring isolation exercise is named separately in the L AIC program. (Manual p.45, 74)
biceps_femoris.context: BF produces tibial ER whenever it contracts — that's anatomy, not pathology. PRI's claim is that BF is overactive as a compensator, increasing tibial ER beyond normal magnitude. The clinical observation (foot turned out) is the net sum of rotations across the hip and knee; femoral ER from piriformis or other deep rotators compounds with BF's tibial ER. The medial hamstrings are the corrective target because semimembranosus and semitendinosus produce tibial IR during knee flexion, directly opposing BF's pattern. This distinction is tested: 'corrective hamstring work' in PRI means medial hamstrings. (Manual p.2, 45; rotational compounding is anatomical, not PRI)
biceps_femoris.hierarchyRole: Inhibited as a compensator. Corrective target is medial hamstrings (semimembranosus / semitendinosus), not BF itself.
